incorrects.description: Student selected 5, confusing items per group with number of groups

params.description: All 3 boxes highlight to emphasize container count.

steps.text: You counted what's inside. The question asks how many BOXES. Count the containers, not what's in them.

incorrects.description: Student selected 8, possibly adding groups and items

params.description: All 3 boxes highlight.

steps.text: Groups means how many containers you see. Don't add. Just count the boxes.

incorrects.description: Student selected 15, counted total items instead of groups

params.description: All 3 boxes highlight to emphasize the containers.

steps.text: You counted all the markers. We need the number of GROUPS. A group is one box. How many boxes?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Boxes highlight sequentially: 1, 2, 3.

steps.text: Let me show you. When we count groups, we count the CONTAINERS. Watch: 1 box, 2 boxes, 3 boxes. Three groups. The answer is 3 because there are 3 boxes.

incorrects.description: Student selected 3, confusing number of groups with items per group

params.description: First box opens to reveal 5 markers inside.

steps.text: That's how many boxes there are. Now look INSIDE one box. Count what's in there.

incorrects.description: Student selected 8, possibly adding groups and items

params.description: First box opens to reveal markers inside.

steps.text: We're not adding. Look inside ONE box. How many markers are in that box?

incorrects.description: Student selected 15, counted all items instead of items per group

params.description: First box opens, showing markers inside.

steps.text: You counted all the markers. We need how many in EACH group. Look at just one box. Count those.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: First box opens to reveal 5 markers.
Markers inside the first box highlight sequentially: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.

steps.text: Here's how to find items in each group. Open one box and count what's inside. Watch: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Five markers in this box. The answer is 5 because each box contains 5 markers.

incorrects.description: Student set container count to 3 (reversed the numbers) on first or second attempt

params.description: Container count control highlights.

steps.text: You set 3 bags. But we need 2 GROUPS. The first number tells you how many bags to make. What's the first number?

incorrects.description: Student set container count incorrectly on first attempt (not the reversed case)

steps.text: Check the prompt. How many groups?

incorrects.description: Student set container count incorrectly on second attempt (not the reversed case)

params.description: Container count control highlights.

steps.text: The prompt says 2 groups of 3. The first number is the groups. That's how many bags. Set 2.

incorrects.description: Student set container count incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System sets container count to 2. 2 empty bags appear.

steps.text: Let me show you. When we say 2 groups of 3, the first number tells us how many groups. That's 2. So we need 2 bags. Each bag is one group.

incorrects.description: Student set items per container to 2 (reversed the numbers) on first or second attempt

steps.text: You set 2 in each bag. But we already have 2 bags. The second number tells you how many go INSIDE. What's the second number?

incorrects.description: Student set items per container incorrectly on first attempt (not the reversed case)

steps.text: Look at the prompt again. How many in each group?

params.description: Items per container control highlights.

steps.text: The prompt says 2 groups of 3. The second number is how many in each bag. That's 3. Set 3.

incorrects.description: Student set items per container incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System sets items per container to 3. 3 items appear in each of the 2 bags.

steps.text: Let me show you. When we say 2 groups of 3, the second number tells us how many go in each group. That's 3. So each bag gets 3 items. Now we have 2 groups of 3.

incorrects.description: Student selected 2 × 4, reversed factors

params.description: All 4 circles highlight to emphasize the group count.

steps.text: Count the circles. That's how many groups you have. The groups number goes first in the expression.

incorrects.description: Student selected 4 + 2, used addition instead of multiplication

params.description: Items in one circle highlight.

steps.text: You have equal groups here. When we have groups with the same amount in each, we multiply. Look: 4 circles, 2 dots each. That's 4 × 2.

incorrects.description: Student selected 2 + 4, used addition instead of multiplication

params.description: Items in one circle highlight.

steps.text: You have equal groups here. When we have groups with the same amount in each, we multiply. Look: 4 circles, 2 dots each. That's 4 × 2.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Circles highlight sequentially as they are counted: 1, 2, 3, 4.

steps.text: Let me show you. First, count the circles: 1, 2, 3, 4. That's 4 groups.

params.description: Dots in one circle highlight: 1, 2.

steps.text: Now look inside one circle: 1, 2. Two in each group. So it's 4 groups of 2. That's 4 × 2. The groups number comes first, then what's in each group.
